title: Should the last weaver teach a machine?
summary: Argue whether a dying handicraft should be kept exactly as it has always been or allowed to change so it can survive, weighing authenticity against extinction.
topic: Should a dying craft be kept unchanged or allowed to modernise so it survives?
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (400–500 words) arguing whether the last masters of a traditional craft (for example hand-weaving) should keep it exactly as it has always been done, even if it dies with them, or change it — new tools, new products, new buyers — so it survives in an altered form. Weigh authenticity against extinction. Use present perfect at least once, a conditional at least once, and 'although' or 'however' at least once.
hintStarters: When the last person who knows how to do something dies, the question is not only what is lost, but…
Although a craft that changes to survive is no longer exactly the craft it was, the alternative…
If the weavers refused every modern tool and every new design, the result might be…

hintVocab.meaningVi: một nghề thủ công đang mai một
tính nguyên bản, tính chân thực
giữ cho điều gì đó tồn tại
kiến thức của nhiều thế hệ
đánh mất linh hồn của nó

hintVocab.phrase: to earn a living from a craft
hintVocab.meaningVi: kiếm sống bằng một nghề thủ công
làm cho điều gì đó loãng đi, kém đậm đà

sampleText:
In a village in the hills, perhaps a dozen women still weave cloth on wooden looms the way their grandmothers did, counting threads by memory and dyeing the wool with plants they gather themselves. The youngest of them is past sixty. Their children have moved to the cities, and almost none of the cloth sells for what the labour is worth. Within twenty years, unless something changes, this particular way of weaving will simply stop existing — not because anyone decided to end it, but because the last hands that know it will grow still. The question the village faces is uncomfortable: is it better to let the craft die exactly as it was, or to change it enough that it might live?

There is a strong case for keeping it pure. The cloth means something precisely because it is made the old way; the slow gathering of plants, the patterns held only in memory, the small irregularities of a human hand. Replace the natural dyes with chemical ones, swap the wooden loom for a faster machine, and you may keep the look while losing the thing itself. I understand this view, and part of me shares it. A craft that has survived for centuries deserves more respect than to be quietly modified into something easier to sell. However, I have come to believe that this purity, taken to its limit, is a beautiful way of choosing death.

If the weavers accepted no change at all, the craft would end up perfectly preserved and completely dead — a tradition that no living person practises is not a tradition but an exhibit. A photograph of a loom, however careful, teaches no one to weave, and a pattern written in a book is not the same as a pattern held in a pair of working hands. I have seen a better path in a neighbouring village, where younger weavers have started selling online and designing for modern homes while still weaving by hand on the old looms. They kept the part that is genuinely hard to replace — the technique, the eye, the patience — and changed only the part that was killing them, which was who they sold to and how. They changed the product so that the core technique stays in living hands. Change is not the enemy of a craft; the enemy is the moment no one alive can do it anymore. A craft that bends can be passed to the next pair of hands; a craft that refuses to bend can only be photographed, and a photograph, in the end, weaves nothing.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect for an achievement that still holds today
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'A craft that has survived for centuries' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành với 'for + khoảng thời gian' để nói về điều gì bắt đầu trong quá khứ và vẫn còn tồn tại đến hiện tại. Nó nhấn mạnh chiều dài và sự liên tục của truyền thống.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'A craft that has survived for centuries' uses the present perfect with 'for + duration' to describe something that began in the past and still exists today. It stresses the length and continuity of the tradition.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Second conditional to argue the cost of refusing change
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If the weavers accepted no change at all, the craft would end up perfectly preserved and completely dead' dùng câu điều kiện loại 2 (if + past simple, would + V) để chỉ ra hậu quả giả định của một lựa chọn — một cách lập luận mạnh mẽ mà không cần khẳng định nó đã xảy ra.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If the weavers accepted no change at all, the craft would end up perfectly preserved and completely dead' uses the second conditional (if + past simple, would + base verb) to spell out the hypothetical consequence of a choice — a powerful way to argue without claiming it has already happened.

sampleTranslationVi:
Ở một ngôi làng trên đồi, có lẽ khoảng một chục người phụ nữ vẫn dệt vải trên những khung cửi gỗ theo cách bà của họ đã làm, đếm sợi bằng trí nhớ và nhuộm len bằng những cây cỏ họ tự hái. Người trẻ nhất trong số họ đã ngoài sáu mươi. Con cái họ đã chuyển ra thành phố, và gần như không tấm vải nào bán được đúng với giá trị của công sức bỏ ra. Trong vòng hai mươi năm, trừ khi có điều gì thay đổi, cái lối dệt đặc biệt này sẽ đơn giản là ngừng tồn tại — không phải vì có ai quyết định chấm dứt nó, mà bởi những đôi tay cuối cùng biết về nó sẽ ngừng lại. Câu hỏi mà ngôi làng phải đối mặt thật khó chịu: liệu để cho nghề chết đi đúng như nó vốn có thì tốt hơn, hay thay đổi nó đủ để nó có thể sống?

Có một lập luận mạnh mẽ cho việc giữ nó thuần khiết. Tấm vải mang ý nghĩa chính bởi vì nó được làm theo lối cũ; sự thu hái cây cỏ chậm rãi, những hoa văn chỉ được giữ trong trí nhớ, những điểm không đều nhỏ của một bàn tay con người. Thay thuốc nhuộm tự nhiên bằng thuốc nhuộm hóa học, đổi khung cửi gỗ lấy một cái máy nhanh hơn, và bạn có thể giữ lại vẻ ngoài trong khi đánh mất chính bản thân nó. Tôi hiểu quan điểm này, và một phần trong tôi đồng tình với nó. Một nghề đã tồn tại qua nhiều thế kỷ xứng đáng được tôn trọng hơn là bị âm thầm sửa đổi thành thứ gì đó dễ bán hơn. Tuy nhiên, tôi đã đi đến chỗ tin rằng sự thuần khiết này, khi đẩy đến giới hạn của nó, là một cách chọn cái chết đầy mỹ miều.

Nếu những người thợ dệt không chấp nhận thay đổi nào cả, nghề thủ công sẽ kết thúc trong trạng thái được bảo tồn hoàn hảo nhưng hoàn toàn chết — một truyền thống mà không một người sống nào thực hành thì không phải truyền thống mà là một hiện vật trưng bày. Một bức ảnh chụp khung cửi, dù cẩn thận đến đâu, cũng không dạy được ai dệt vải, và một hoa văn ghi trong sách thì không giống một hoa văn được giữ trong một đôi tay đang làm việc. Tôi đã thấy một con đường tốt hơn ở một làng lân cận, nơi những người thợ dệt trẻ hơn đã bắt đầu bán hàng trực tuyến và thiết kế cho những ngôi nhà hiện đại trong khi vẫn dệt bằng tay trên những khung cửi cũ. Họ giữ lại phần thực sự khó thay thế — kỹ thuật, con mắt, sự kiên nhẫn — và chỉ thay đổi cái phần đang giết chết họ, đó là họ bán cho ai và bán như thế nào. Họ thay đổi sản phẩm để kỹ thuật cốt lõi vẫn nằm trong những đôi tay đang sống. Sự thay đổi không phải kẻ thù của một nghề thủ công; kẻ thù là khoảnh khắc không một ai còn sống có thể làm được nó nữa. Một nghề biết uốn mình có thể được truyền cho đôi tay tiếp theo; một nghề từ chối uốn mình thì chỉ có thể được chụp ảnh lại, và một bức ảnh, rốt cuộc, chẳng dệt được gì.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the present perfect to describe long survival continuing to now?
options: A craft that survived for centuries since now deserves respect.
A craft that has survive for centuries deserves respect.
A craft that is survived for centuries deserves respect.
A craft that has survived for centuries deserves respect.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect 'has survived' with 'for + duration' fits something continuing to the present. Option 2 adds the contradictory 'since now' to a past simple. Option 3 uses the base verb 'survive'. Option 4 uses 'is survived', a passive that does not fit an intransitive verb here.
mcQuestions.hint: Present perfect = has + past participle ('survived'); pair it with 'for + duration'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a second conditional correctly to argue the cost of refusing change?
options: If the weavers accepted no change, the craft would die perfectly preserved.
If the weavers accepted no change, the craft will die perfectly preserved.
If the weavers would accept no change, the craft would die perfectly preserved.
If the weavers accept no change, the craft would die perfectly preserved.
mcQuestions.explanation: Second conditional = if + past simple, would + base verb. Option 2 uses 'will'. Option 3 puts 'would' in the if-clause. Option 4 mixes present simple with 'would'.
mcQuestions.hint: Second conditional: if + past simple, then would + base verb.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'however' correctly to introduce a contrasting view?
options: I share that view, however taken to its limit it becomes a way of choosing death.
I share that view. However, taken to its limit it becomes a way of choosing death.
I share that view however, taken to its limit it becomes a way of choosing death.
I share that view; however taken to its limit it becomes a way of choosing death.
mcQuestions.explanation: When 'however' starts a new sentence as a contrast, it takes a full stop before and a comma after. Option 2 joins two clauses with just a comma before 'however' (comma splice). Option 3 misplaces the comma. Option 4 omits the comma after 'however'.
mcQuestions.hint: Sentence-initial 'however' needs a full stop (or semicolon) before it and a comma after it.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option most naturally describes a tradition still actively practised?
options: an alive tradition
a lived tradition
a living tradition
a life tradition
mcQuestions.explanation: 'A living tradition' is the natural collocation. 'Alive' is not used attributively before a noun like this. 'A lived tradition' wrongly uses the past participle. 'A life tradition' uses the noun 'life' as if it were an adjective.
mcQuestions.hint: Which form of 'live' goes directly before a noun to mean 'still active'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'the way + subject + did' to describe an inherited method?
options: They weave the way their grandmothers were.
They weave the way how their grandmothers did.
They weave the way like their grandmothers did.
They weave the way their grandmothers did.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'The way + subject + did' is the correct, concise structure. Option 2 uses 'were', which leaves the verb 'weave' unexpressed and sounds wrong. Option 3 adds a redundant 'how'. Option 4 adds a redundant 'like'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'The way someone did' needs no extra 'how' or 'like'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence most precisely describes a craft that exists only as an object on display?
options: It is not a tradition but an exhibit.
It is not a tradition but an exhibition.
It is not a tradition but an exhibitor.
It is not a tradition but an exhibiting.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'An exhibit' is a single item on display, which fits the meaning. 'An exhibition' is the whole event or show. 'An exhibitor' is a person who shows things. 'An exhibiting' is not a noun.
mcQuestions.hint: Which word means a single object displayed in a museum?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'so that' to express purpose?
options: They change the product so that the core technique stay in living hands.
They change the product so that the core technique stays in living hands.
They change the product so for the core technique stays in living hands.
They change the product in order the core technique stays in living hands.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'So that + clause' with correct agreement ('stays') expresses purpose. Option 2 uses 'stay', breaking subject-verb agreement. Option 3 uses 'so for', which is not a connector. Option 4 omits 'to' from 'in order to' / 'in order that'.
mcQuestions.hint: Use 'so that' plus a clause with a verb that agrees with its subject.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence most naturally describes a craft adapting to keep going?
options: The craft adapted for survive.
The craft adapted surviving.
The craft adapted to survive.
The craft adapted of surviving.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Adapt to + base verb' (infinitive of purpose) is correct. Option 2 uses 'for survive'. Option 3 drops the link and uses '-ing'. Option 4 uses 'of surviving', which is unidiomatic here.
mcQuestions.hint: Use the infinitive of purpose: 'adapted to survive'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly contrasts two outcomes using parallel relative clauses?
options: A craft that bends can be passed on; a craft that refuses bending can only be photographed.
A craft that bends can be passed on, a craft what refuses to bend can only be photographed.
A craft that bend can be passed on; a craft that refuses to bend can only be photographed.
A craft that bends can be passed on; a craft that refuses to bend can only be photographed.
mcQuestions.explanation: Both relative clauses are parallel and grammatical, joined by a semicolon. Option 2 uses 'refuses bending' instead of 'refuses to bend'. Option 3 uses 'what' instead of 'that' and a comma splice. Option 4 uses 'that bend' without subject-verb agreement.
mcQuestions.hint: Keep the two clauses parallel: 'refuses to bend', subject 'craft' takes 'bends'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best states the article's central claim clearly and naturally?
options: Change is not the enemy of a craft; the enemy is the moment no one alive can do it anymore.
Change is not the enemy of a craft, the enemy is the moment no one alive can do it no more.
Change is not the enemy of a craft; the enemy is the moment no one alive can does it anymore.
Change is not enemy of a craft; the enemy is the moment no one alive can do it anymore.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option 1 uses a semicolon, the single negative 'anymore', and correct verb forms. Option 2 has a comma splice and a double negative ('can ... no more' after another negative). Option 3 uses 'can does'. Option 4 drops the article in 'not enemy'.
mcQuestions.hint: Use a semicolon, 'the enemy', a single negative, and 'can do' after the modal.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The sample claims that a craft which refuses to change at all is 'a beautiful way of choosing death'. Do you agree that survival in an altered form is better than a pure ending? Is there a point at which a changed craft has changed so much that it would be more honest to let it go?
